title: Aladdin and the Magic Lamp
summary: In a far city of China, though the tale itself comes from the sands of Arabia, there lived a poor tailor named Mustapha and his wife, who had one son.
full_text:
In a far city of China, though the tale itself comes from the sands of Arabia, there lived a poor tailor named Mustapha and his wife, who had one son. That son was Aladdin, and he was, by all accounts, a hopeless scoundrel. From morning till night he ran through the streets with other idle boys, playing knuckle-bones and dice, and refusing every attempt his father made to teach him the tailor's trade. His father died of grief, and still Aladdin did not mend his ways.

One day, when Aladdin was fifteen, a stranger approached him in the street. He was a tall man with a dark face and piercing eyes, and he wore the robes of a magician from the distant land of Morocco. He claimed to be Aladdin's long-lost uncle, returned after forty years abroad. Aladdin's mother, poor and desperate, believed the lie. The stranger showered them with gifts and promises.

The magician led Aladdin deep into the mountains beyond the city gates, through gardens and rocky valleys, until they reached a narrow gorge between two peaks. There the magician lit a fire, threw strange powders into the flames, and muttered incantations that made the earth tremble. The ground split open, revealing a stone slab with a brass ring. Terror seized Aladdin, but the magician struck him and said: "Obey me, and you will find a treasure greater than any king possesses."

Aladdin lifted the stone and saw steps descending into darkness. The magician gave him a ring and told him to fetch an old oil lamp from a niche at the bottom of the cave. Aladdin descended through three vast halls filled with gold, crossed a garden of jewel-laden trees, and found the lamp. But as he climbed back, the magician demanded the lamp be handed up first. Suspicious, Aladdin refused. The magician flew into a fury, sealed the cave entrance with magic, and left Aladdin trapped in the darkness.

For three days Aladdin sat in despair. At last, in desperation, he rubbed the ring the magician had given him. A monstrous genie appeared, filling the cavern with his presence. "I am the Slave of the Ring," the genie boomed. "Command me." Aladdin asked to be taken home. In an instant, he stood in his mother's house.

When his mother tried to clean the old lamp to sell it, she rubbed it, and a far greater genie emerged, towering to the ceiling. "I am the Slave of the Lamp," said the genie. "Your wish is my command." Aladdin asked for food, and a feast fit for a sultan appeared on silver platters.

Over time, Aladdin used the lamp to lift his family from poverty. One day he saw the Sultan's daughter, Princess Badroulboudour, and fell hopelessly in love. He sent his mother to the palace with jewels from the garden of the cave, and the Sultan, dazzled by such wealth, agreed to the marriage. The lamp's genie built Aladdin a magnificent palace of marble and jade, with twenty-four windows studded with rubies and emeralds.

But the Moroccan magician learned of Aladdin's fortune. Disguised as a merchant trading old lamps for new, he tricked the princess into exchanging the magic lamp. The genie transported the entire palace to Africa. Aladdin, heartbroken, used the ring genie to travel to his wife. Together they plotted. The princess feigned welcome to the magician, drugged his wine, and Aladdin killed him. Recovering the lamp, Aladdin brought his palace and princess back to China. They lived in peace and prosperity, and Aladdin proved that even a worthless boy could become a great man, not through magic alone, but through courage and cleverness.

title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves
summary: In a town in Persia, there once lived two brothers.. The elder, Cassim, married a wealthy woman and became the richest merchant in the marketplace.
full_text:
In a town in Persia, there once lived two brothers. The elder, Cassim, married a wealthy woman and became the richest merchant in the marketplace. The younger, Ali Baba, married a woman as poor as himself and earned his living by cutting wood in the forest and selling it from the backs of three donkeys.

One afternoon, as Ali Baba was loading his donkeys with firewood, he saw a cloud of dust rising on the horizon. A band of horsemen approached at full gallop, their saddlebags bulging. Fearing they were robbers, Ali Baba climbed a large tree and concealed himself among the branches. From his hiding place, he counted forty men. They dismounted before a massive rock face, and their captain strode forward and called out: "Open, Sesame!"

To Ali Baba's astonishment, a door swung open in the solid rock. The forty thieves entered, and the door closed behind them. After a time, they emerged, remounted their horses, and rode away. When the coast was clear, Ali Baba climbed down, approached the rock, and spoke the magic words. The door opened into a cavern filled with gold coins, silken fabrics, gem-encrusted weapons, and sacks of silver. He loaded his donkeys with as much gold as they could carry and hurried home.

Ali Baba's wife needed to measure the gold, so she borrowed a measuring cup from Cassim's household. Cassim's wife, curious about her poor sister-in-law's sudden need for a measure, secretly smeared the bottom of the cup with lard. When the cup was returned, a gold coin was stuck to it. Cassim confronted Ali Baba and forced him to reveal the secret of the cave.

The greedy Cassim rode to the cave with ten mules. Inside, he filled sack after sack with treasure. But when he tried to leave, the magic words would not come to his lips. In his excitement, he had forgotten them. "Open, Barley!" he cried. "Open, Wheat!" The rock stayed shut. The forty thieves returned, found him inside, and cut his body into quarters as a warning.

When Cassim did not return, Ali Baba went to the cave and found his brother's remains. He brought them home and entrusted them to Morgiana, his brother's clever slave-girl. Morgiana was as quick-witted as she was loyal. She found an old cobbler named Baba Mustapha, blindfolded him, and led him to the house to sew the quarters of Cassim's body back together, so that a proper burial could be held without arousing suspicion.

The thieves, finding the body gone, knew someone else shared their secret. One by one, they tracked down the cobbler and, by offering gold, convinced him to retrace his steps. The first thief marked Ali Baba's door with chalk, but Morgiana spotted the mark and chalked every door in the street. The second thief used red chalk, and Morgiana did the same. Both thieves were killed by their captain for failure.

Finally, the captain himself memorized the location and devised a plan. He disguised himself as an oil merchant and arrived at Ali Baba's house with forty mules, each carrying a large clay jar. Thirty-nine of the jars concealed armed thieves; only one held oil. The captain begged shelter for the night.

Morgiana, always watchful, noticed something strange. When her oil lamp ran low, she went to the jars. The first thief inside whispered, "Is it time?" Morgiana, realizing the plot, answered in a low voice: "Not yet." She saw the danger. She filled a great cauldron with oil, boiled it, and poured enough into each jar to kill every hidden thief. The captain, discovering his men dead, fled into the night.

Later, the captain returned disguised as a merchant and befriended Ali Baba's son. He was invited to dinner. Morgiana recognized him despite the disguise and, during the feast, performed a dagger dance that ended with the captain's heart stilled. For her courage and loyalty, Ali Baba freed Morgiana and married her to his son. Together they lived in prosperity, and Ali Baba was the only man alive who knew the words that opened the cave.

title: Scheherazade
summary: The chronicles of the Sassanian kings tell of a monarch named Shahryar, who ruled an empire stretching from India to China.
full_text:
The chronicles of the Sassanian kings tell of a monarch named Shahryar, who ruled an empire stretching from India to China. He was a just and capable king, beloved by his subjects, but his heart was a wasteland of betrayal. After his first wife's infidelity, Shahryar concluded that all women were deceivers. He devised a terrible punishment: each night he would take a new bride, and each morning at dawn he would have her executed. For three years, the kingdom mourned as daughter after daughter was taken to the palace and never seen again.

At last, the grand vizier, whose duty it was to provide these brides, could find no more maidens. He sat in his chambers with his head in his hands, certain that the king would have him killed for failing. He had two daughters of his own: Scheherazade, the elder, and Dinarzade, the younger. Scheherazade was not only beautiful but had studied philosophy, medicine, poetry, history, and the sciences. She had read a thousand books and forgotten nothing.

Seeing her father's despair, Scheherazade approached him with a steady voice. "Father, I have a plan. Give me to the king as his next bride." The vizier recoiled in horror. "Have you lost your senses? You know what awaits you at dawn." But Scheherazade was resolute. "I have read the histories and the tales of the ancients. I know that stories have power. If I die, I die gloriously, trying to save my people."

She took her sister Dinarzade aside and whispered instructions. "When I am in the king's chambers, I will ask that you be allowed to sleep in our room. An hour before dawn, you will wake me and beg me to tell one last story. Do this, and I will begin a tale so compelling that the king will want to hear its end."

The wedding was held that evening. As Scheherazade was brought before the king, she wept, and Shahryar asked the cause. She begged permission for her sister to spend the night in their chamber, so that she might bid farewell. The king agreed. In the darkness before dawn, Dinarzade spoke: "Sister, if you are not asleep, please tell us one of your beautiful stories to pass the last hour of the night."

Scheherazade turned to the king. "Will your majesty permit me?" He nodded, curious despite himself. And so she began to speak. She told a tale of a merchant who made a terrible bargain with a genie, and the three old men who came to his aid with stories of their own. She wove the words like silk, and the king listened, spellbound. As the first light of dawn crept through the window, she reached the most exciting part and stopped.

"The story is not finished," she said. "But the morning has come." The king, desperate to know what happened next, delayed her execution for one day. The next night, she finished the tale and immediately began another, leaving it even more suspenseful than the last.

And so it went for a thousand and one nights. She told the story of Aladdin and the magic lamp, of Ali Baba and the forty thieves, of Sinbad the sailor's seven voyages, of lovers and princes, of magical horses and enchanted islands. Each night she stopped at dawn, her life hanging on the king's desire to hear just one more story.

During those three years, she bore the king three sons. And on the morning after the thousand and first night, she presented her children before Shahryar and said, "O King, I have given you sons. If you still wish to kill me, do so, but know that I have loved you."

The king looked at his children and at the woman who had changed him through the power of her words. His heart had softened over those nights, not through magic, but through the steady revelation of humanity, beauty, and truth that her stories had unfolded. He revoked his decree. The kingdom rejoiced. And Scheherazade, who had saved an entire generation of women through the power of storytelling, lived as queen in honor for the rest of her days.

title: Antar and Abla
summary: The epic romance of Antarah ibn Shaddad, a 6th-century pre-Islamic Arab poet-warrior born a slave to an Ethiopian mother and Arab father in the tribe of Banu Abs. Despite his dark complexion (one of the 'Arab crows'), Antar rose through courage and poetry to win his freedom and the hand of his cousin Abla. His poem became one of the Seven Hanging Odes (Mu'allaqat) displayed in the Kaaba. The romance spans his heroic battles, his eloquence, and his enduring love for Abla against tribal opposition. The saga, comparable to Arthurian legend, remains central to Arab oral tradition and is still recited in coffeehouses across the Middle East.

title: The Seven Hanging Poems (Mu'allaqat)
summary: A collection of seven long pre-Islamic Arabic poems considered the finest examples of ancient Arabian poetry, traditionally said to have been written in gold on Coptic linen and hung from the Kaaba in Mecca. The seven poets are Imru' al-Qais, Tarafa, Zuhayr, Labid, Antarah, Amr ibn Kulthum, and Harith ibn Hilliza. Each poem exemplifies the qasida form with themes of love (nasib), desert journeys, tribal boasting, and moral wisdom. The Mu'allaqat represent the pinnacle of pre-Islamic oral tradition and served as the linguistic and literary benchmark for subsequent Arabic poetry.

title: Aladdin and the Wonderful Lamp
summary: A poor young ne'er-do-well in a Chinese city is recruited by a Maghrebi sorcerer to retrieve a magic lamp from a booby-trapped cave. When the sorcerer betrays him, Aladdin discovers a genie bound to the lamp that grants wishes. He becomes wealthy, marries Princess Badroulbadour, and builds a magnificent palace. The sorcerer returns to steal the lamp by trickery, but Aladdin uses a magic ring to recover it. Though added to the Arabian Nights by Antoine Galland from Syrian storyteller Hanna Diyab, it became one of the most famous Middle Eastern tales worldwide.

title: Sinbad the Sailor (Seven Voyages)
summary: A wealthy Baghdad merchant known as Sinbad the Sailor recounts his seven extraordinary voyages to a poor porter named Sinbad the Landsman. Each voyage brings shipwreck, monstrous creatures (the giant bird Roc, gigantic serpents, whale-islands, cyclopean giants), and fantastic lands. Sinbad outwits cannibals, escapes the Valley of Diamonds, rides sea monsters, and encounters the Old Man of the Sea who clings to his shoulders. Through wit, courage, and Allah's mercy, he returns home each time richer, only to be driven again by wanderlust. The tales are a masterpiece of Arab maritime folklore.

title: The Fisherman and the Jinni
summary: A poor fisherman casts his net four times daily. On the fourth cast, he hauls up a copper jar sealed with Solomon's seal. Opening it releases an enormous Ifrit (jinni) who, having languished in captivity for centuries, has resolved to kill whoever frees him. The fisherman tricks the jinni back into the jar by questioning how such a mighty being could fit inside, rescaling it. After the jinni pleads and promises rewards, the fisherman releases him. The jinni leads him to a magical pond with talking fish, setting off a chain of revelations about an enchanted prince turned half to stone.

title: The City of Brass
summary: Caliph Abd al-Malik sends Emir Musa and his party across the Sahara to find the legendary City of Brass, built by King Solomon to imprison rebellious jinn. They encounter a brass horseman statue that points the way, a chained Ifrit imprisoned in a pillar, and an ancient city with walls eighty cubits high and twenty-five gates, eerily preserved but inhabited only by corpses. Inside lies the embalmed princess Tadmurah of the Amalekites, her treasure guarded by animated statues. Greedy Talib ignores warnings and is decapitated by guardian statues. The party retrieves brass bottles containing imprisoned jinn and returns to Damascus.

title: Layla and Majnun
summary: Based on the true 7th-century story of Qays ibn al-Mulawwah, a Bedouin poet of the Banu Amir tribe in Najd, who fell deeply in love with his cousin Layla. When her father refused the match due to Qays's obsessive behavior (seen as madness or jinn-possession, earning him the epithet 'Majnun' meaning 'crazy'), and forced Layla to marry another man, Qays fled to the desert, living among wild beasts and composing heartbroken poetry. He wasted away and was found dead among stones near Layla's grave. The story became the archetypal tale of unconsummated, spiritual love across Persian, Turkish, and Urdu literature, widely called the 'Romeo and Juliet of the East.'

title: Hatim al-Tai: The Legend of Generosity
summary: Hatim ibn Abdallah of the Tai tribe, a pre-Islamic Arab chief in 6th-century Yemen, became the enduring symbol of Arab generosity and chivalry. Countless legends recount his extreme hospitality: slaughtering his prized horse when no other food was available for guests, giving away his possessions until left with nothing, and offering his own life to an assassin sent by a jealous king rather than sending the man back empty-handed. Even after death, legend says he hosted travelers from beyond the grave. The name 'Hatim' remains synonymous with generosity across the Arab and Persian world, and his son Adi became a companion of the Prophet Muhammad.

title: Iram of the Pillars (Ubar, the Atlantis of the Sands)
summary: A legendary lost city in the Arabian desert mentioned in the Quran, built by the tyrannical King Shaddad son of Ad in an attempt to create an earthly paradise with gold and silver ramparts, pillars of chrysolite and ruby, and rivers flowing through channels of gold. God destroyed Shaddad and his entire army with a mighty sound when they were one day's march from the city, blotting out the road forever. The city became known as the 'Atlantis of the Sands.' In 1992, satellite radar imagery led to the discovery of a 2,000-year-old fortified trading site at Shisr in Oman, now identified by some with the legendary Ubar.

title: The Anqa (Mythical Bird of Arabia)
summary: The Anqa (or Anqa Mughrib) is a golden, fabulous female bird in pre-Islamic Arab mythology, described as very beautiful and colorful with a long neck, human face, four pairs of wings, and a whiteness in its neck. She lives alone on Mount Qaf, flies far away, and only appears once in ages. Created by God with all perfections, she became a scourge (hence the word 'anqa also means calamity) and was exterminated by a pre-Islamic prophet. She lives 1,700 years, mates at 500, and her chick stays in the egg for 125 years. Often identified with the Persian Simurgh and the phoenix, in Turkish she is called Zümrüdü Anka (the Emerald Anqa).

title: The Ghoul of the Desert
summary: In pre-Islamic Arabic folklore, the ghul (ghoul) is a demonic, shape-shifting creature inhabiting deserts and graveyards. Usually appearing female to lure unwary travelers, the ghul has donkey-like hooves, a feline head, forked tongue, and hairy skin. It calls victims by name in the voice of a loved one, then devours them. The poet Ta'abbata Sharra famously boasted of killing a ghul in single combat. Described by al-Jahiz and in the Arabian Nights, ghouls are a type of jinn that feast on human flesh and corpse meat. The word entered English through William Beckford's 1786 novel Vathek.

title: King Solomon and the Jinn
summary: According to Islamic and Arabic folklore, Prophet Solomon (Sulayman) was granted dominion over all beings: mankind, jinn, birds, wild beasts, and the wind. He commanded armies of jinn and devils, imprisoned rebellious marids and ifrits in brass bottles sealed with molten lead and his signet ring, and cast them into the seas. He understood the speech of birds and animals, and his throne was carried by the wind. His vizier Asaf possessed the knowledge to bring the Queen of Sheba's throne instantly. Solomon's ring, bearing the Most Great Name of God, was the source of his power, and when lost to a demon named Sakhr, his kingdom was briefly usurped.

title: The Tale of the Three Apples
summary: A classic murder mystery from the Arabian Nights. Caliph Harun al-Rashid orders his vizier Jafar to solve the murder of a young woman found dismembered in a chest. The investigation leads to her husband, who confesses after returning home to find a strange apple in her possession and killed her in jealous rage. However, the actual chain of events involves a slave who had taken the apple from the caliph's own palace. Jafar is given three days to find the killer or face execution. The story is notable as an early example of the 'locked room' mystery structure and detective fiction.

title: Qamar al-Zaman and the Princess of the Jinn
summary: A tale from the Arabian Nights about Prince Qamar al-Zaman (Moon of the Age), son of King Shahriman, who refuses marriage and is imprisoned. The princess of the jinn, in a parallel story, also refuses marriage and is imprisoned by her father the jinn king. Through the machinations of a devious jinn and a fairy, they are magically brought together in sleep, each seeing the other in dreams. They fall in love sight unseen, leading to a quest across the realms of men and jinn to unite. The story exemplifies the supernatural romance genre of medieval Arabic literature.

title: Rostam and Sohrab
summary: The most famous tragedy of the Persian Shahnameh. Rostam, Iran's greatest hero, travels to Samangan where he marries Princess Tahmineh. He returns to Iran, leaving her pregnant with Sohrab. Years later, Sohrab, now Turan's mightiest warrior, leads an army against Iran, hoping to find his father. Unaware of each other's identity, father and son meet in single combat. After a grueling battle, Rostam throws Sohrab and stabs him. Only then does Sohrab show the armband amulet given by Tahmineh. Rostam discovers he has killed his own son. Despite desperate pleas for medicine, Kay Kavus withholds it. Sohrab dies in his father's arms, and Tahmineh burns all her possessions in grief.

title: Zahhak: The Serpent-Shouldered Tyrant
summary: Zahhak (Aži-Dahāk) was a prince seduced by Ahriman (the devil) who kisses his shoulders, causing two black serpents to sprout. They demand daily sacrifices of young men's brains for sustenance. Zahhak overthrows the just king Jamshid and rules Iran for a thousand years with cruelty. The blacksmith Kaveh, having lost several sons to the serpents, leads a revolt and raises his leather apron on a spear (the Derafsh Kaviani flag) as a symbol of resistance. The noble Feraydun ultimately defeats Zahhak, chains him in Mount Damavand, where he remains imprisoned until the end of the world. The legend originates in Zoroastrian texts (Avesta) and is central to the Shahnameh.

title: Simurgh and Zal
summary: Sam of Zabulistan abandons his albino infant son Zal on Mount Alborz, ashamed of his white hair. The mythical bird Simurgh, a wise, benevolent creature resembling a giant dog-bird, finds the baby and raises him as her own. Zal grows into a handsome, powerful youth. Sam has a dream of reproach and seeks forgiveness. Zal returns to his father but receives three Simurgh feathers to burn when in need. Later, Zal marries the beautiful Rudabeh, but she nearly dies in childbirth. Zal burns a feather, and Simurgh appears to perform the first known cesarean section, saving both mother and child — the child being Rostam, Iran's greatest hero.

title: Kaveh the Blacksmith and the Derafsh Kaviani
summary: Kaveh, a humble blacksmith, loses all but one of his sons to the daily sacrifice demanded by King Zahhak's serpent-shoulders. He storms the royal court, refusing the king's bribe of signing his remaining son's death warrant. Kaveh raises his blacksmith's leather apron on a spear as a battle standard and leads the populace in revolt. He seeks out Feraydun, the rightful heir, and guides him to overthrow Zahhak. The leather apron, embellished with jewels over centuries, became the Derafsh Kaviani, the legendary imperial standard of Iran used until the Arab conquest. Kaveh symbolizes righteous popular rebellion against tyranny.

title: Rostam's Seven Trials (Haft Khan-e Rostam)
summary: To rescue King Kay Kavus, captured by the White Demon (Div-e Sepid) in Mazandaran, Rostam undertakes seven impossible labors: (1) Killing Rakhsh's attacker, a ferocious lion; (2) Surviving a waterless desert with his horse Rakhsh; (3) Slaying a monstrous dragon; (4) Killing a powerful witch through divine discernment; (5) Capturing the warrior Olad to serve as guide; (6) Defeating the giant Arzhang Div; and (7) The ultimate trial — battling and killing the White Demon (Div-e Sepid) himself in the demon's cave, using the demon's own liver to restore the blinded king's sight. This epic is the Persian equivalent of Hercules' labors.

title: Bijan and Manijeh
summary: Bijan, a knight of Iran and son of Giv, is sent to drive wild boars from Armenian fields. Afterward, the envious knight Gorgin tempts Bijan to cross into enemy Turan by describing the beautiful gardens of Afrasiab. Bijan meets Manijeh, the daughter of Afrasiab, and they fall in love. She drugs him and smuggles him into her chambers, but Afrasiab discovers them and chains Bijan in a dungeon. Manijeh is exiled to wander the wilderness. The Iranian king Kay Khosrow sees Bijan in his crystal cup and sends Rostam disguised as a merchant into Turan. With Manijeh's help, Rostam frees Bijan and returns him to Iran, where Manijeh becomes his wife.

title: Khosrow and Shirin
summary: The Persian prince Khosrow Parviz falls in love with the Armenian princess Shirin through a portrait painted by his friend Shapur. Their love is thwarted by a series of obstacles: Khosrow's forced marriage to the Byzantine princess Mariam for political alliance, his dalliance with Shekar in Isfahan, and the tragic love of the sculptor Farhad who carves Mount Behistun for Shirin's sake (and dies upon hearing false news of her death). After Mariam's death, Khosrow finally marries Shirin. But Khosrow's son Shiroyeh, who also desires Shirin, murders his father. Shirin, to avoid marrying Shiroyeh, takes poison and dies on her husband's body. They are buried together.

title: Hushang Discovers Fire (Sadeh Feast)
summary: The second king of the Pishdadian dynasty in Persian myth, Hushang, grandson of the first man Gayomard, one day hurls his flint axe at a serpent blocking his path. He misses the snake but the axe strikes a rock, producing a spark. Curious, the king strikes two flints together and creates fire deliberately — the first controlled fire in human history. He proclaims it a divine gift, makes fire his altar, and establishes the Sadeh feast to celebrate the discovery. Hushang also teaches humanity metalworking (fashioning axes, saws, adzes), irrigation, agriculture, animal husbandry, and fur clothing, fundamentally transforming human civilization.

title: Bahram Gur (Bahram V, the Hunter King)
summary: The Sassanid king Bahram V (Bahram Gur), renowned for his love of hunting (especially onager, giving him the epithet 'Gur' meaning onager), is the subject of numerous Persian legends. His exploits include hunting with a lion and an onager as companions, his romance with the Indian princess Sapinud (whom he marries after killing a lion and a dragon), and his building of the seven pavilions for his seven princesses (the subject of Nizami's Haft Paykar). Bahram embodies the ideal Sassanid king: martial prowess, justice, and cultural patronage. The story of 'Bahram and the Dragon' recounts his killing a dragon to save a beautiful maiden.

title: Siyavash and Sudabeh
summary: Prince Siyavash, son of Kay Kavus, rejects the seductive advances of his stepmother Sudabeh. To prove his innocence, he rides through a mountain of fire and emerges unscathed. Despite this vindication, he cannot remain in Iran and voluntarily exiles himself to Turan, where King Afrasiab initially welcomes him and gives him his daughter Farangis in marriage. Suspecting conspiracy, Afrasiab orders Siyavash's execution, and the prince is beheaded. This unjust death becomes the casus belli for generations of war between Iran and Turan, and Siyavash's blood is avenged by his son Kay Khosrow. The story is one of the Shahnameh's most profound tragedies, exploring honor, betrayal, and fate.

title: The Al (Childbirth Demoness)
summary: In Persian folklore, the Al is a demonic being personifying puerperal fever, targeting women during and after childbirth. The name derives from Iranian 'al' meaning red, and she is described as a red-haired, stunted female creature with a rudimentary black humanoid form. She attacks mothers within the first six days after birth, causing sickness (al-zadagi) and death. Belief in the Al is widespread across Iran, the Caucasus, and Central Asia, with parallels in Jewish Lilith, Mesopotamian Lamaštu, and Armenian al traditions. Talismanic scrolls (hmayils) invoking saints are used to ward her off. She steals livers of newborn infants and must be neutralized by piercing her nose.

title: The Peri (Fairy Spirits of Persian Myth)
summary: Peris are beautiful, benevolent winged spirits in Persian mythology, originally fallen angels in Zoroastrian tradition who later became more benign in Islamic Persian folklore. They are depicted as exquisite beings who dwell in the lower heavens and enchant humans with their beauty. Peris are locked in eternal conflict with the malevolent Divs (demons), who often capture and imprison them in cages. Unlike divs (who are male), peris are usually female and friendly to humans. Queen of Sheba (Bilqis) is considered offspring of a Chinese emperor and a Peri. The word 'peri' entered English through Thomas Moore's 1817 poem 'Lalla Rookh' ('Paradise and the Peri').

title: The Akvan Div (The Demon of Madness)
summary: In the Shahnameh, the Akvan Div is a fearsome demon of madness who terrorizes Iran. King Kay Khosrow sends Rostam to destroy him. The demon finds Rostam sleeping and, instead of killing him outright, tosses him into the sea (a trick of his mad nature). Rostam swims ashore and, using cleverness, tricks the Akvan Div into revealing where he keeps his external soul. In a final confrontation, Rostam decapitates the demon, whose severed head continues to speak and curse. The Akvan Div represents the chaotic, irrational forces that threaten the civilized order of Iran, and his defeat demonstrates that brute force alone cannot overcome madness — cunning and intelligence are required.

title: Mam and Zin (Mem û Zîn)
summary: The Kurdish national epic, written down in 1692 by poet Ahmad Khani. Mem, a chivalrous youth of the Alan clan and heir to the City of the West, falls in love with Zin, the beautiful daughter of the governor of Botan (Cizre). They meet during a Newroz celebration. Their union is blocked by the jealous Bakr, who conspires against them. Mem is poisoned, and Zin, upon learning of his death, collapses and dies of grief at his grave. They are buried together, but a thorn bush grows between their graves, separating the lovers even in death — the roots of malice penetrating deep into the earth. Bakr is killed by Mem's friend Tacdin and buried nearby, his malice persisting as the thorn bush. The epic is foundational to Kurdish literature and identity.

title: Kawa the Blacksmith and Newroz
summary: The foundational Kurdish myth explaining the origins of Newroz (the Kurdish New Year on the spring equinox). The evil Assyrian king Zahhak (Zuhak) had serpents growing from his shoulders that demanded daily sacrifices of young men's brains. Kawa, a blacksmith who lost all but one of his sons to the sacrifice, rallied the people in revolt. He led them to the mountains, forged weapons, and stormed Zahhak's palace, killing him with his blacksmith's hammer. Kawa then lit a bonfire on the mountaintop to signal victory — a fire that spread across the land, melting winter and heralding spring. The Derafsh Kaviani (Kawa's leather apron on a spear) became the symbol of the uprising. Today Kurds celebrate Newroz by lighting bonfires and jumping over flames.

title: The Epic of Dimdim (Kela Dimdimê)
summary: A Kurdish heroic epic based on the historical siege of Dimdim Castle (1609-1610) in the Bradost region near Lake Urmia. The Kurdish leader Emir Khan Lepzerin (Xanê Lepzêrîn, 'Gold-Arm Khan') rebuilt the ancient fortress of Dimdim on a 2,000-meter mountain. The Persian Safavid Shah Abbas the Great, seeing the growing Kurdish power, sent his grand vizier Hatem Beg to besiege the fortress. The Kurds held out for seven years against the Persian army. When Dimdim finally fell, the Persians massacred all defenders. The epic is sung by Kurdish dengbêj (traditional storytellers) and is considered the second most important Kurdish national epic after Mam and Zin. It symbolizes Kurdish resistance against foreign domination.

title: Mount Cudi and Noah's Ark (Kurdish Tradition)
summary: According to Kurdish creation myths, Noah's Ark landed on Mount Cudi (Cudi Dagh) in southeastern Turkey, not Mount Ararat as in the biblical tradition. The Kurdish tradition holds that after the flood, Melik Kurdim, a member of Noah's tribe, established rule over Mount Cudi and built the first post-diluvian civilization. He created the Kurdish language, considered the first non-Semitic language. The city of Judi was the first town built after the Flood, followed by the castles of Sinjar and Silvan. This tradition is recorded by the 17th-century Ottoman traveler Evliya Çelebi. Mount Cudi has been a pilgrimage site for millennia, with Assyrian rock carvings 2,700 years old providing archaeological evidence of ancient pilgrimage.
